full_response:
Let the origin 0 be Allie’s starting point and let x∈ℤ\{0} be the (unknown) position of the crumb.  
Allie executes the following search pattern

for n = 1,2,3,…  
 walk +n inches from the origin, return to the origin;  
 walk –n inches from the origin, return to the origin.

She stops as soon as she reaches the crumb.

Definition of competitiveness  
An on-line searching strategy is k-competitive if for every possible crumb position x

(total distance walked by the strategy to reach the crumb) ≤ k · |x|.

We must show that for the above “1,1,2,2,3,3,…’’ strategy no finite k satisfies the inequality, i.e. the strategy is not competitive.

-------------------------------------------------
Cost incurred when the crumb is at position d>0
-------------------------------------------------

(Because of symmetry, the same analysis applies to d<0.)

• While n < d  
 Allie completes both the +n and –n excursions, incurring a cost of 2n (out and back) for each excursion.
• When n = d  
 Allie starts the +d excursion, walks d inches and lands on the crumb; she then stops.

Hence the total distance walked is

T(d)=∑_{n=1}^{d-1}2n (forward excursion) + ∑_{n=1}^{d-1}2n (backward excursion) + d  
  = 4 · (1+2+…+(d-1)) + d  
  = 4 · (d-1)d/2 + d  
  = 2d(d-1)+d  
  = 2d² – d.

(The optimal omniscient ant would of course walk only |d|=d inches.)

-------------------------------------------------
Competitive ratio achieved on input d
-------------------------------------------------

R(d)= T(d)/d = (2d² – d)/d = 2d – 1.

-------------------------------------------------
Unboundedness of the ratio
-------------------------------------------------

Let k be any positive constant.  
Choose d ≥ (k+2)/2 (d can be taken to be any integer of that size). Then

R(d)=2d – 1 ≥ 2·((k+2)/2) – 1 = k + 1 – 1 = k.

Thus, for that choice of d,

distance walked by the strategy = R(d)·d ≥ k·d,

contradicting k-competitiveness.  
Because this can be done for every k, no finite competitive ratio exists.

(The same calculation with d replaced by –d shows the result for crumbs to the left of the origin.)

-------------------------------------------------
Conclusion
-------------------------------------------------

Allie’s “1 inch out and back, 1 inch the other way and back, 2 inches out and back, …’’ search strategy has competitive ratio 2|x|−1, which grows without bound as |x| increases. Therefore no constant k can upper-bound the ratio, and the strategy is not competitive.

\boxed{\text{Allie’s linear-increment search is not competitive; its competitive ratio is }2|x|-1\to\infty.}

Confidence: 0.86
extracted_answer: \text{Allie’s linear-increment search is not competitive; its competitive ratio is }2|x|-1\to\infty.
